title: Why Linux Runs the Cloud
description:
## The Default OS of Infrastructure

A new engineer asks why nearly every cloud server, container base image, and CI runner they encounter is running **Linux** rather than another operating system.

Which answer best explains Linux's dominance in server and cloud infrastructure?

options.text: Linux is open source and free to run at scale, extremely stable, deeply scriptable from the shell, has a rich package ecosystem, and containers are built on Linux kernel features — so it's the natural fit for servers and the cloud.
Linux requires a per-core paid license for every server, which large clouds prefer because it simplifies billing.
Linux is popular mainly because of its polished desktop GUI, which administrators use to click through server configuration.
Servers need Windows-only features such as the registry and Active Directory, and Linux emulates them better than Windows itself.

explanation: Linux dominates server and cloud infrastructure for several reinforcing reasons: it is **open source and free** to deploy across thousands of machines (no per-instance license cost), it is **stable and lightweight** for long-running headless workloads, it is **deeply scriptable** through the shell and tools so everything can be automated, it has a mature **package ecosystem** (apt, yum/dnf, etc.), and — crucially — **containers are a Linux-native technology** built on kernel features like namespaces and cgroups. Option b is false: mainstream Linux distributions don't require a per-core license (you may pay for *support*, but the OS itself is free). Option c is wrong because servers are typically managed *without* a GUI — administration is done over SSH and scripts, and many server installs have no desktop at all. Option d is backwards: those are Windows features, and you wouldn't run a Linux server to emulate them.

hints.title: Why do containers need Linux?
hints.body: Containers rely on Linux kernel primitives (namespaces and cgroups) for isolation. That alone ties the entire container/cloud ecosystem to Linux.
